CURRENT AFFAIRS.

It is remarkable how little difference in the daily
lives of people political programmes, or, what is more
of a reality, political activity, make. If is the daily

ives of the people that make up the sum total of the
life of the nation. How many men, not to say women,
_have altered the even temor, or the bustle, of their
daily routine since the Irish Party issued its manifesto,

- and since the National Directory published its resolu-
ons? In these days when some people are excited,
and when the country as a whole remains in a state of
ing very much, it is wholesome to keep an

People will

I‘%{k nd be merry, if bhey have the focans who-

o the fact from themselves: any..

no likelihood of people getting
v ate er our vanous combmatxons

.breadth than depth and intensity.

of tailors of Tooley Street may do. The Irish Parlia-
mentary Party are not highly efficient as a diplomatic,
or as a fighting, machine. In the rough and tumble
way in which a political party is gathered together,
wire- pulling, planting more or less bogus branches, and
the rest of it, it would be idle to look for a very high
level of emclency in any political Party. We feel little
or no resentment against the Party, though neither do
we feel any great amount of confidence in it. It can prob-
ably be said of it that if it were disbanded to-morrow
and a brand new Party elected in its place, that the
new Party would not be much better, even if as
good as the old. So where is the use of grumbling
overmuch at its want of efficiency. "If the specialised
politicians were more efficient we would probably have
got something in the way of legislation to go on with;
but it can be as truly said that if our workingmen were
more efficient, and our manufacturers, and our news-
papers, and our farmers, we would be far better off.
Ireland is an inefficient country; and the great work
is to make her efficient; mere policies, or programmes
won’t do that; and it is rather a case of the workman
quarrelling with his tools for an inefficient people to be
either making a scapegoat or a panacea of this policy
or that.

We do not look forward to any great rise in real
pollucal activity, as we cannot see whele the driving
power is to come from. We are too well off, and the
safety valve of emigration for all classes is too con-
venient, to permit us generating the forces of real down-
right desperation. It was the economic motive behind
the Land League that chiefly made it formidable. We
have no hopes of this country, in its present state of
development, putting up a real good fight for any idea
except an idea connected with religion, and heaven
knows we are lax and confused enough in the latter
regard. The young men in the debating societies who
grow to hate one another hotly on account of differences
¢oncerning.the ultimate goal, or the true definition of
a final settlement, are only subjects for comedy; they
are. not real factors, though they are distractions.
Where, then, is the force to come from that is to form
a ‘“really virile agitation?’ The workingmen of Ire-
land are largely anglicised, and hitched on to Man-
chester and other English centres; the farmers who are
secured on their land, or are buying it out, will grumble
and pass resolutions, but where is the motive to fight?
Patriotism? People who know the farmers know that
they are patriotic very much within limits.  The
attempt to break up the ranches in parts of Ireland is
an economic movement, and is, so far, a real thing, but
it is only partial and confined to certain districts. The
over-taxation depends largely for its success on an all-
Ireland combination, and such a combination, if happily
it should come about, must, from its nature, have more
We think that the
specialised politicians are deceiving themselves if they
think that they can get up a real formidable frontal
attack after the recent debacle. ‘

The first resolution passed by the National Directory
stated, amongst other things:— We repeat our
couutry s demand for an elected Legislature, controllmg
Irish domestic affairs, and an Executive responsible to
that Legislature; and we urge a speedy and united ex-
pression of national determination to secure these rights,
and not rest content with less.”’  After the recent

" political debacle, that is certainly a bold pronounce-

ment; but the height of high comedy is reached when
the ‘ Extreme’’ camp, under the championship of Mr.
Edward Martyn, held a meeting and denounced this
whlttlmg down of the national demandl The*

treme " Party unanimously passed the following:—

Resolved—That this Council repudiates the definition
of the Trish demand adopted by the Directory of ‘the
United Trish League, viz., a statutory legislature de-
barred from control of Imperial taxation, Excise, Cus-
toms, the Consular and Diplomatic service, Army and
Navy, and questions of peace and war; and declares
1_5_11@ bhe_ minimum Irish demand is a sovereign Parlia-
ment, with powers equal and co-extensive with those of
the Parliament of Great Britain.” Tt s excellent
comedy, and it is comic to think that no one would
see the comedy of it better than Mr. Martyn were it not
for the accident that he is acting the part of stage king
in the piece! The “ Extreme ’’ Party asit was making
a move might have suggested whether Mr. John Sweet-
man or Alderman Cole should be admiral of the navy.
Mr. Synge’s ‘“ Playboy ’ was objectionable and gro-
tesque, but the “ Playboys” of Mr. Martyn’s Party
are really amusing. Have they dropped ¢ the floor of
the Hague?’

To return to the National Directory—What practical
effect can a series of meetings in support of the demand
for Self-Government have? We are all of us in favour
of Self-Government, and what is the use ‘at the present
time demonstrating the fact. The problem is, how can
we hit the enemy? Reiterating our'demands is reitera-
tion rather than political activity. What is the use of
talking of impressing on ‘the minds of ‘the English
Statesmen.”” So far as English Statesmen go it is
dynamics that count; if you can squeeze them, squeeze,
if not, ‘‘reiterating our demands’’ and that sort of
thing is only play-acting that at this time of day
will probably not even deceive the very green people,
not to talk of deceiving England.

Now, if instead of attempting to operate on “the
minds of the English Statesmen,”” they turned round
and tackled the real enemy, the enemy within our
gates, ‘“ England’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland,” as
Brother Goulding styled them, they would show States-
manship and prove that they meant business. But we
are, unfortunately, convinced that the specialised poli-
ticians are afraid to stand up to ‘“ England’s Faithful
Garrison.”” If we had a daily paper we would shortly
put the fear of justice into the heart or gizzard of that
Party; but the *‘ Freeman ’’ won’t do it: it is afraid
to fight the ‘“ Irish Times’’; and, as for Mr. William
Murphy’s self-styled ‘‘ nationalist organ,’”’ the ‘‘ Inde-
pendent,”” we read in that paper that the King, when
he goes to the Exhibition, is to be received by, amongst
others, Mr. W. M. Murphy! So the ‘ Independent
is not likely to take on Sir John Arnott and ‘“England’s

"Faithful Garrison’’ yet awhile.

We are glad that the Directory instructed that

the over-taxation robbery should be agitated,
it will be something to have ‘“a full clear
statement of the facts prepared for circula-
tion amongst League branches in TIreland and

Great Britain,”” though we cannot readily recognise ‘‘ an
active and practical campaign ’ in the mere suggestion
that ‘“ strong resolutions of protest, and specches in sup-
port, thereof, be features of every Nationalist demonstra-
tion.”” If the Directory had instructed that the present
temperance movement should he supported by the pass-
ing of ““ strong resolutions *’ and the making of cloquent
speeches against-the immoderata consamption of ‘ strong
drink,’” upon which so much that goes to run up the
over-taxation total is collected, it would he pertinent

and ‘to the point. 4
timid of fighting Mr. Bung as they are of coming to
close quarters with ““England’s Faithful Garrison in

Ireland.”

Needless to say, we heartily approve of the directions
to ‘“ the branchg]s’ of the United Irish League all over:
Treland to make the support of Home Industries a
leading plank of the National platform ; to pass resolu-
tions in favour of the Industrial Revival Movement, at
frequent intervals; to use their influence as an organisa-
tion and individually in this direction, and to give
practical encouragement by word and deed to In}ih
enterprise in every shape and form ; and we instruct t.he
Standing Committee to prepare a memorandum on t sf:
whole subject for circulation amongst the branches o
the United Irish League and all populm‘ly elected
bodies in Ireland.” We are glad of this move on the
part of the Directory, but we have yet to be convinced
that it will materialise into any -ap:
in support of the Industrial Revival. To commence
with, perhaps the men of the West and of other places
would support Irish-made roll tobacco against the Im-
perial Combine ; perhaps the dairy farmers, who use up
the foreign preservatives, would, at least, go to ‘the
trouble of dropping a line ‘to the Irish Preserve Co:,
Trinity Chambers, Cork, and test a sample. However,
though we are not sanguine of great results from this
move of the Directory, we are glad that the move is to
be made. Certainly many of the men who form the
rank and file of the United Irish League, badly want
education in their duty to Irish industry ; the townsmen
helped them in the land fight, and, at least, they might
sec that they get Irish-made roll tobacco and other
things by way of helping the townsman to live in

We likewise are glad to find that the Directory calls

upon the members of the United Irish League Branches, .

whose children are at elementary and secondary scheols,
to insist upon having the Irish language included in
the children’s course of study; and we strongly advise
the organisers of public meetings to secure the atten-
dance of Trish speakers where at all possible.”” We
hope the parents in the United Irish League will answer
to the call.

The political situation in Ireland chiefly arises out of
the -fact that England usurps power in Ireland. The
Trish aim at regaining that power. The main note of
Trish politics is, consequently, international.  Unless
the politicians can put up a good fight against England—
we assume it to be quite useless to hope that they will
pluck up courage to fight ‘ England’s Faithful Gar-
vison in Ireland,”” the real effective enemy—politics are
necessarily in a state of slump in Ireland, and, so far as
we can see, the country lacks at present even the poten-
tial forces for a vigorous agitation against England;
and all talk about a virile agitation, well meant though,
we have no doubt, it is, sounds utterly hollow in cur
cars. Having miserably failed in the attempt to get
IHome Rule by instalments, it seems to us that the
only course lef for ithe politicians is to do all they can
to strengthen and increase the Flome Rule we have by
attempting to get instalments of power such as a Uni-

versity séttloment, or ovicted Tenarts’ Bill, and 'such -

like. That, of course, leaves a rather limited field for
the politicians pure and simple, for the using of the
Home Rule we have is a domestic matter, and is not,
in the Irish sense, politics at all. Tt is rather of the

appreciable movement

THE LEADER.

ture of the work being done by the new Department,
the Gaelic League, the Temperance Societies, the Indus-
trial Associations, etc. Such work does not properly
fall within the scope of politics in Ireland.  The
specialised politicians can, of course, give great help to
~ all these various forms of organised activity and develop-
ment ; but as Trish politicians called into being to fight
English usurpation in Treland, they are not primarily
concerned with works of Irish self-development. And
if the politicians swing too far towards the work of
Irvish self-development, they will tend to cease to be
Irish politicians, and become a sort of newer Depart-
ment—but a Department without a Government Grant !

The Cloghaneely College, Gortahork, Co. Donegal, is
to be opened on Wednesday, July 3vd. Such a means
of acquiring Irish in an Irish district should be
especially welcome to national teachers not already cer-
tificated, as the Board have consented to accept the cer-
tificates of the college in lieu of those of the Board’s
inspectors. Ten free scholarships are offered, one for
each Ulster County, and one for Meath. These include
free tuition, board, and lodging for four weeks. Stu-
dents from Cavan, Monaghan, Fermanagh and Louth
will be examined on 30th June, at Castleblayney Feis;
those from North Ulster at Derry on 2nd July or Clog-
haneely on 4th July; those from Meath at Kells Feis on
30th June.

A correspondent writes to us:—“ I would be very
glad if you would lend your powerful aid in respect of
the Mining Industry of Iveland. There are in this
locality very rich copper deposits which await develop-
ment, and which only want proper legislation to enable
them to be dealt with. The land has been purchased
under the Land Act of 1903, and the Mineral Rights
are vested in the Land Commission, who have them-
selves no power to deal with them. If things were the
same here as in any other country, they would have
been working long ago, and perhaps would enable
plenty of our countrymen to stay at home. By insert-
ing the above you will, perhaps, be the means of interest-
ing some of our M.P.’s in the auestion who can do a
good deal in Parliament to facilitate the matter.”

At a recent weekly meeting of the Library and Tech-
nical Tnstruction Committee of the Corporation of Bel-
fast, a mgmorial, containing eighteen signatures, was
laid before the Committee requesting that a Gaelic class
be established in the Institute. Irish is as a red rag to
a bull to the anti-Irish bigots of Belfast. A local
reader writes to us as follows on the matter :—‘ In your
issue of the 22nd September, 1906, you commented on
the prospectus of the Belfast Technical Institute which
contained eight ‘Idolatrous’ teachers out of a staff

f seventy-four.  You also expressed surprise that
:here wasynot an Irish class, although French, taught
by a Swiss (I suppose for purity of accent), German and.
Spanish were in full swing. Il\To“{ you will see by en-
closed cutting that the old spite is still strong. Is it
not a scandal that there are classes for Irish in Londpn
and Manchester Technical Schools, but_ in this Irish
city the native tongue is tabooed. Hoping for a word
in the Leaper which may lead the powers that be to
mend their ways.””

Alderman Sir James Henderson presided at the meet-
ing, and the proposal to have Irish taught was so revolt-
ing to that sensitive gentlemen that he favoured the
meeting with a display of anti-Ivish bigotry. })l{rmg
the discussion, Alderman Byrne very rightly l'usls('.ed
that the Committee should not act on prejudices as
they were the trustees of the ratepayers. Evidently it
was no use appealing to such persons as Sir James
Henderson to put their prejudices in their pocket, for
that titled anti-Irishman is reported to have remarked:
““ Let them learn it in some other place. I know there
are seven or eight classes in the city for the teaching
of Gaelic. Let the priests teach it.”” The anti-Irish
prejudice won on a division, only two members, Alder-
man Byrne and Councillor Mackin, voting for Irish.
There’s the ““ Irish ”’ city of Belfast, and toleration and
impartiality for you!

We published a letter last week from a young Irish-
man who had just passed into the Civil Service and who
expected to be sent to either London or Edinburgh.
We stated that we could not say whether or not there
was a branch of the Gaelic League in Edinburgh. A
correspondent has since kindly informed us that there
is a branch, the Thomas Davis branch, of the Gaelic
League in Edinburgh.

Some recent promotions in the
Board Offices tell their own tale of the ascendancy
spirit at work. A vacancy for a staff officer was
created by the death of Mr. J. F. Mayne. The only
Englishman in the higher grade of the second division
in the office, a Mr. A. Jenkins, was shovelled into the
vacancy. The post carries a salary of £350 rising to
£450. The Englishman was shovelled over the heads of
five mere Irish papists, all of whem, we understand, are

senior to Mr. Jenkins in length of service, and are
indeed staff officers on a scale lower than that to which
the English Mr. Jenkins has been promoted. Their
salaries are £300 rising to £400, and ‘in the ordinary
course one of these mere Irish papist officials should have
been promoted to the higher salary now enjoyed by
‘Mr. Jenkins. But Mr. Jenkins is an Englishman and
does not fall under the withering influences of His
Majesty’s coronation oath. A Mr. Monsell, another
gentleman who escapes the malediction of His Majesty’s
coronation oath, was promoted to be a staff officer at'a
salary rising from £250 to £350; he was promoted over
the heads of four ‘‘ Idolators.’” "Another gentleman,
one Mr. R. Eckersley—a non-Irishman, as his name
would suggest, who was, until recently, the only English-
man in the lower grade of the second division, was pro-
moted to be a minor staff officer—salary from £150
rising to £250: his salary at date of promotion was
£107 10s. 0d., going up by ordinary increments of
£7 10s. 0d. to £190, and then by £10 to £250. The
case of Mr. Eckersley is, perhaps, the most glaring of
the three. He was -a junior assistant to Mr. Jenkins,
and when the English Mr. Jenkins was promoted, his
senior assistant, a papist of 15 or 16 years’ service,
should have been promoted to Jenkins’ place and have
been made an officer of the higher grade of the second
division at a salary of £250 rising to £350. Instead,
a new ppst was created for the English Mr. Eckersley,
and he was placed senior to five *“ Idolatrous * TIrish-
men, some of whom hold University degrees. It is
probable that the ascendancy party, who evidently have
a rather free hand at the Local Government Board
Offices, were suffering from slight panic at the prospect
of the recent Irish Council’s Bill being passed into law
and made a spurt.

In connection with the Irish Training College of Bel-
fast, or what is known as the ‘‘Scoil Ghaedlach,”’ it
-has been decided, in view of recent facilities regarding
the teaching of Irish and the giving of certificates for
same, to call a conference of school managers and
teachers, and of others interested. The conference will
be held in the rooms of the above institution, 5,
Murray’s Terrace, Belfast, on Thursday, 27th inst., at
8 p.m., and will consider, amongst other things, ways
and means for still further extending the scope and use-
fulness of the Scoil, and in particular, for interesting
a larger number of teachers in Belfast and surrounding
districts in the work. As the new school year com-
mences on July 1st, when the new scale of fees for Irish
comes into operation, it is probable that the question
of the more general introduction of Irish into the
schools of the district will also receive attention at the
meeting.

On Sunday next the formal opening ceremony at the
Munster Training College, Colaiyoe na Murhan, of
Ballingeary, Co. Cork, takes place. It is expected that

a number of prominent Gaelic Leaguers of Munster will
be present on the occasion. The work of the College

will begin on the following day, Monday, and so, also,

will the Summer School in connection with the College.
The latter is intended for those whose knowledge.of
Trish is limited, and who wish to have the opportunity
of mixing with native speakers. An tAthar Rlsﬁe;?.rd
0 Dala, whose remarkable series of articles now running
in the LEaDER, on ‘‘Irish Spelling Simplified,”” are
attracting such widespread attention, is drd Ollamh,
the other professors are an Tataip o Nudliain, of
St. Malachy’s College, Belfast, Diarmuid O’Leary, and
Dr. Osborne Bergin, of the School of Irish Learning.
Applications from intending students have come from
all parts of Munster, and from Dublin, chl_ilow, Kil-
kenny, Belfast, Galway and England, and quite a large
number of the applicants came from National teachers.

Concerning the glaring case of exclusive dealing and
boycotting on the part of the agent of the Duke of
Devonshire’s Lismore Estate which we exposed recently,
and about which we published a letter last week from
an ‘“ Old Tenant,”” we have received the following from
the Very Rev. Archdeacon T. McGrath, P.P., V.F,,
of Lismore:—* Referring to the incident detailed by an
€Old Tenant’ in the boycotting of Catholics by the
Lismore Estate Office officials, T regret ever so much
the necessity for my interference. The harmony that
had hitherto existed between the different creeds is
sundered by the virulent bigotry of the Estate officials
as brought to light by you in the LEeapEr of the 15th
June. I should certainly have been satisfied with hav-
ing made my protest against such unjust and un-
charitable proceedings to the Duke of Devonshire in
person, thereby having done my duty in this painful
matter, and, as I thought, allowing it to rest there.
You have now, however, made it public property, and
the cases put on paper by an ‘ Old Tenant’ and given
to me were five in number (and one other quoted making
six), were put before His Grace, insisting that there could
be no mistaking the progress and success of the effort to
supplant the Catholics by the introduction of pen-
sioners of any kind or class provided they were not
Catholics. How the protest was received, tramseat for
the present: The ¢ Old Tenant’ meanwhile insists that
the trouble has been brought by the misapplied zeal
of a few evangelizing spinsters ready for any mischief in
the spread of the faith that is in them.”’

II.—DOMESTIC ECONOMY.

AST week we introduced our readers to the Glas-

L nevin Farm where they teach all about live
stock, crops and fruit growing. As you have to catch
your hare before you cook it, it was quite in proper
order to go to the Glasnevin College Farm before we
went to the Kildare Street School of Domestic Economy.
The Most Rev. Dr. Kelly, Bishop of Ross, has en-
deavoured to impress on the public mind the wastage of
national material wealth caused by the partial want of
skill in utilizing the raw household material to the best
advantage. This national leakage is plain to all obser-
vers who know Ireland from the inside. We will not
dwell now on the causes beyond remarking that the
standard of comfort—if the phrase is not an irony in
this connection—of millions of us was driven so low
by forces beyond our own control that it was not the
art of cooking potatoes, but of procuring them, that was
a trouble to us. Habits and points of view arising out
of these circumstances tend, naturally, to harden into
traditions and prejudices, even as we go on and prosper
comparatively. But it were time that we ran a plough
through these crusted traditions. ; :
The Kildare Street School of Domestic Science wa:
originally the local habitation of a voluntary school for
private students. Since the Department took it over
some years ago, all the regular students are winners of
scholarships, and the school is utilized mainly, indeed
almost wholly, for the training of ladies who intend to
qualify for the work of instructing in domestic economy.
There were 31 students on the roll when we visited the
school one day recently. The school is carried on

IS ENGLAND FIT ¥OR HOME RULE ?
(Extracts from Royal Commission Report).

N obedience to the request of Your Majesty, we have
conductesl an exhaustive inquiry into the suita-
bility, or otherwise, of your English subjects for a
measure of Self-Government, similar to that lately
granted to the Colonies. We have examined a number
of witnesses, drawn from different parts of the country,
in reference thereto. Our unanimous finding is: That
the English people are totally unsuited for the respon-
sibilities of Self-Government.
The criminal statistics of England would alone con-
firm the wisdom of our decision. Allowing even for
density of population in the large cities and towns, the
number of indictable offences is altogether in excess of
that ~ of neighbouring portions of Your Majesty’s
dominions. The public morale of these cities and towns
is evidently below that of uncivilised races. . . . .
We have had before us witnesses from Sheffield, who
gave expression to the opinion that the police force of
that city ought to be increased to five times its present
strength. - The county forces, they maintained, should
also be augumented, for the number of agrarian crimes,
such as cattle-maiming, in any given county is greater
_than for any equi-sized county in Ireland. (Supplemen-
tary mnote). It has come under our notice, too, that
where the English influence prevails, outside England,
a similar state of low public morality is to be found, v.
Mr. Wm. Moore, M.P.’s recent remarks on the morality
of N. E. Ulster.)

Seeing that the volume of opinion favours an aristo-
cratic form of government, we cannot refrain from draw-
ing Your Majesty’s attention to the state of higher
society, and the need of reform and purer living amongst
the wealthier classes of your English subjects before we
could recommend the devolution of even a minimum of
governmental power.

‘We have examined a considerable number of election
petitions, and observed that in Worcester and Corn-
wall (amongst others) the elections were won by bribery,
treating and such-like illegalities. We are confident
that were we to pursue our investigations further, a

barely tolerated. ~ We think that the rights of this
latter minority must be safeguarded by introducing
into the composition of any mew Legislative Chamber, a
nominated element of about 24 members drawn from
the Catholics of Treland.

D We find, too, that considering their edu-
cational facilities, the English people are very deficient
in branches of ordinary knowledge, and, generally, in
matters of interest outside those of their own country.
The parson is often the only well-educated man in the
parish, and, being such, takes his place as the natural
leader of the people. Hence we must conclude that the
people are parson-ridden, and so altogether unfit for the
duties and responsibilities of Self-Government. They
can only be introduced to the principles of  self-re-
spect ”’ (vide Birrell’s speech), by carefully graduated
legislation.

We consider that the inherent defects of Protes-
tantism—the prevailing religion of the country—exer-
cises an unfortunate influence on the general character
of the people. £

Lastly, we are committed against Self-Government
for the reason that the whole nation is disloyal to the
throne and person of Your Majesty. The existence of
Jacobite clubs has been brought under our notice.
Besides, there are many thousands of Socialists and
Revolutionists, as well as hundreds of Scottish and
Welsh Separatists. Lendon is the hot-bed of Anar-
chists, Nihilists and Communists. The ultimate objeet
of all this disloyalty, no matter how cleverly concealed, is
Sep- the dissolution of the constitution. . . .
We cannot advise Your Majesty’s Government to any
course than the one, the resolute maintenance of the
Union. W. G. FaLLon.

IRELAND KOT AT WORK. 2

FROM NAVAN TO CASTLETOWN.

IT 5o happened that at Broadstone Station, on our

way to Navan, we fell in with Mr. Anthony Scott,
‘the well-known ‘architect, one of whose sons is following
in the professional footsteps of his father, and has
already earned fame as the designer of the Spiddal
church. When we had inspected Navan at work, we
went back to Mr. Anthony Scott to fulfil an engagement:
to lunch with him. Mr. Scott knows Meath over a
period of twenty or thirty years. He is architect for one
of the few new buildings going on at present in Navan—
the Belfast Bank—and a fine solid building it is. About
eight miles to the North of Navan there are cross roads ;
they are called Castletown. There are four or five
houses and a church, and the church is undergoing
alterations and enlargement. Mr. Anthony Scott is
the architect, and that explains why he drove out there
from Navan, and, accepting his kind invitation to
accompany him, we spent a very pleasant evening, and
a partly meditative evening, in the country where
grass grows, and water runs, and cattle browse.

In the general shop at Castletown, the assistant is a
thorough-going Irish Irelander who learned Irish in
Navan: he is teaching it now in the parish. It was by
learning Trish that he learned he had a country, and
so he is a tragi-comic figure—an enthusiast in a shop at
a cross roads in the county of cows; perhaps there is
more tragedy than comedy in the situation.

Fat Meath is, indeed, a dreary, hopeless sort of
place. The mountain lands of Kerry are congested,
and congestion is better, it is more homely, anyway,
than desolation; the fat lands of Meath are a living
-desolation. From Navan to Castletown it was cattle,
cattle all the way, sheep here and there, an odd ass and
cart driven by a lad, and a few stupid-eyed tramps to
break the monotony of growing grass. Who will write
us ‘“ The Song of the Grass?’ We travelled through
TIreland not at work. Grass grew, tramps tramped, and
cows chewed their cud. Even the cranky nag between
the shafts didn’t like work, and Jack, the driver, who
owned up to seventy-eight, declared that the nag was
““a rogue.”” Perhaps he was only typical of the spirit
that broods over the Meath prairie and did-not like
exertion. The old proverb that necessity has no law, is,
in a way, exemplified on the barren hills of Kerry,
where the people toil, whilst on the Meath prairie
nature is allowed to do mnearly all the work.' At
Williamstown, on our’way back, Mr. Anthony Scott
took us into the house of Mr. Finnegan, a big farmer,
as farming is understood in Meath. Mr. Finuegan is
seventy years of age, a big kindly man, who ought to
know Meath if anyone knows it. ‘‘ It is not the land
for the people we want here,”” said he, “ but the people
for the land.”” Supported by such mature and weighty
authority, we do not mind hazarding the view that that

‘sums up the situation in rural Meath where Ireland is
not at work. There is not much prospect of Meath
wituessing a cattle ‘‘ drive,”” for there does not appear
to be any people to drive them. A few neat labourers’
cotitages, inhabited probably by a few stray herdsmen

and some terriers, are taking the places of the cabins.

of Royal Meath, and, judging by one or two of the
specimens of cabins still standing, one cannot wonder
if the people who lived in them were not sorry to leave
the country.

can read English can be taught to read the Irish of

it be given to them in the present complicated spelling ;
and our readers will have advantages over the average
native speaker, as matters have been up till now, be-
cause they can make the acquaintance of the long,

SOME OF THE IRISH EXHIBITS AT THE

E have heard so much lately of the Ballsbridge
Exhibition being ‘‘ International,”” and we
have been so constantly hearing of the low state of
Trish industries, that it came upon me as a pleasant
surprise to find the present ‘ International ’ Exhibi-
‘tion depending for nearly half its attraction upon the
stands of Irish maunufacturers; and this in spite of the
fact that several Irish firms have, rightly or wrongly,
refused to countenance the International affair.

I intended to make a thorough study of the small
section which, I thought, would represent Ireland in
‘this cosmopolitan conglomeration. But I soon found
myself suffering from what our international friends
‘would call ““ embarras de richesses.”” On entering the
central hall I could see nothing else but Irish
stands; such names -as. Gallagher and Cleeve met
‘my eyes from all points. I went to the last-named

ind, and a little enquiry elicited the fact “that this

erick butter industry gives employment to 1,200
ishmen and Irishwomen, while their sweet factory
eeps 400 busy. Yet there are people still who tell us

t there is nothing for a vigorous young Irishman to

is hand to in his own country. g

I turned down the next wing and found myself in a

aze of distilleries, feebly diluted with a little Irish

d English minerals. Overpowered by the bare idea

f so much ¢ pot-still,’” I sought refuge in the Palace of

vely against the other countries repre-
ak of the soap factories, the

textiles, the colour printers, the

interesting to me to find that two firms in Dublin
(Messs. Stirling and Messrs. Edmond) are sufficiently
enterprising to cut their own corks, instead of paying
Englishmen to do it for them, and to supply such of the
Irish trade as condescend to let their countrymen, and
not others, cut their corks for them. I found twine
made by the Linen Thread Co., and jute and Irish twill
bags done at the Clashawaun Works in King’s Co. In-
cidentally, I was told there is no manufacturer of ftflt
hats in Dublin, a statement I should like to see dis-
proved. The attendant at the City Rubber Stamp Co.’s
stand told me that his was the only Dublin firm which
specialised in rubber stamps, and showed me a fount of
Gaelic type made by them.

The man in the street may be glad to know that
there are, at least, two prosperous coal-mining works in
Ireland—the Arigna Mining Company, and the
Castlecomer Colliery; at the latter’s stand there is a

-working model showing the process of ‘‘screening’’

(otherwise riddling) coal.

Soaps, flours, preserves, and feeding stuffs occupy a
large space in the Palace of Industries. I noticed two
Irish gunners, and one Irish specialist in fishing rods.
At the end of the hall I discovered a curious specimen
of elaborate inlay work from Killarney, half hidden
among Mrs. French’s bog oak and Connemara marble.
Connemara brings me to the Aran and Connemara stall,
where, among the more sophisticated poplin and lace, a
lady showed me strange heavy woollen petticoats hand-
made by Aran girls. These are made, nct for the
market, but for the girls’ own wear; they wear two or
three of them during the Western winters, as well as
a shawl, or maybe two, wrapped round their heads, and
another shawl about the waist. The same lady showed
me men’s woollen suits, heavily cut and curiously
braided. Cowskin Pampootees are another feature of
Aran Tslands industrial activity.

Furnishing of all kinds, especially basket work, seems
to be thriving in the provinces. One exhibiting firm,
from Edenderry, combines furnishing in very beauti-
fully polished elm and ash with carriage-making and
wood-turning ; most of their custom, I was glad to hear,
is inside Ireland, though they have some connection in
London. Delicately-worked china from Belleek, pottery
from Monkstown, and basket-ware from places innumer-
able were other items of interest.

After this large display, it took some energy to inves-
tigate the Home Industries Section ; but the energy was
well repaid, for this section reveals possibilities in the
developmen$ of Irish Tndustries before undreamt of by
the man in the street. Besides the well-known lace and
hose and tweeds, I found a working exhibit of carpet-
making from Donegal, where a lady, tastefully dressed
in black and green, was working the coloured stuff into
the background while her quick eyes followed a pattern
affixed above. Lace curtains from Galway were also
shown in the making, the girl-worker showing me a
specimen she was doing upon the design and under the
instruction of a teacher from the Congested Districts
Board ; the rich yellow lace showed up very effectively

a glove-making exhibit from Fownes, who have started
a factory in Tipperary; and, again, a model tobacco-
curing house, and specimens of tobacco leaves grown in
Mayo and Louth, in Kilkenny, Wexford and King’s
County.

A richly embroidered screen and a few quaint little
wooden toys and ornaments come from Turbostown’s
cottage workers. The most striking among the hand-
made textiles is a bit of damask from Ardoyne.

Two vital suggestions for the development of our
rich industrial resources are to be found in two :ases
set up by the Department of Agriculture; one showing
pottery made of Irish clays tested by that Department;
another giving a finished exhibit of bags made of Irish
hides, brushes of Irish hair, hats of Irish fur, and brush
and knife handles of Irish bone. The suggestion em-
bodied in these two cases should not be overlooked by
those who are genuinely interested in our prosperity as
a nation. If Irish clays and Irish hides, hair, fur and
bones can be made industrial assets, we should set about
making them such at once; the question arises, why w

The possibilities of the finer applied arts in Ireland

We have the Bray wood-carving class showing fine speci-

If my memory serves me correctly, the most striking

Stained-glass windows, by W. J. Douglas and others,

Partners. More wood-carving and a little silk and

There are two cases of book-binding exhibits, showing

of Treland to possibilities of independent life and vigour
and adult beauty; for there is rich quiet beauty about
this enamel work ; a luxuriant harmony of colour against
a dull background of copper or beaten silver, combined
with poetic beauty of conception such as in Mr. Reeve’s
plaque ““ The Falling Star,” or the ‘“ Poet’s Inkpot,’’
by an artist whose name I regret I cannot recollect, or
the brooch, on whose silver background stands a lion,
symbol of passion, cowed by the spirit symbol of renun-
ciation. There is a freshness and force and individuality
about work of this kind which shows a pleasing
aloofness from correctly English standards of art. Mr.
Oswald Reeves, the prime mover in this wavelet of the
great national tide, is concerned, not only with the
various processes of enamelling metals, but also with
beating silver and copper and gold; and his pupils in
the Metropolitan Art School receive the sheet of copper
.or silver, beat it themselves into shape, work in the
colours and put it through the tedious firing processes
till the thing is complete.

This concluded my visit to the Home Industries
section. I had time only for a glance at the working
exhibits, the carding, spinning and weaving of the C.
D. Board, and the carpet-making of the Naas Co-opera-
tive Society. None of the Irish exhibits I found time
to examine as minutely as I would have wished. The
general impression left by a visit of this ‘‘International’’
Exhibition is that there are signs of, at least, the seeds
of vigorous development in Irish industries of the most
varied kinds. If this seed be cultivated as it should be,
we will no longer have our stock excuse for emigration
—that Ireland does not want willing hands and brains;
we need no longer pretend that Ireland is so materially
and spiritually poverty-stricken as to be purely an
agricultural country. M. O’'D.
